assets.name: lecture_04_figure_02.png
assets.rationale: This frame gives the clearest and least occluded view of the full board state. Both time-evolution equations and the orthogonality statement are visible at once, and the lecturer stands far enough left that the mathematical layout remains readable. The later candidates are very similar, but they place the lecturer slightly farther into the writing area without adding any new content.

assets.name: lecture_04_figure_03.png
assets.rationale: This frame gives the best compromise between board completeness and occlusion. The key unitarity heading is fully visible, the top relation is readable, and the two infinitesimal-evolution lines are visible enough to support a cautious reconstruction. Candidate 01 loses more of the right-side heading, while Candidates 03 and 04 place the lecturer more centrally and block more of the equations.
assets.subtitle_excerpt: why you choose one Hamiltonian or Lagrangian

assets.name: lecture_04_figure_04.png
assets.rationale: This is the cleanest and most complete frame in the set. It preserves the full board layout from the infinitesimal evolution formula to the difference-quotient step, with only a minor shadow at the left edge and no substantial occlusion by the lecturer. The later candidates are less useful because the lecturer moves in front of the board while writing the next line.
assets.subtitle_excerpt: The time derivative of psi equals minus i h psi.

assets.name: lecture_04_figure_05.png
assets.rationale: This frame is the best because it captures the finished board state with the final boxed time-derivative equation clearly visible, while the lecturer stands far enough to the left not to block the central mathematics. The surrounding infinitesimal-evolution steps are still readable above it, making the transition from the epsilon-difference quotient to the time derivative especially useful for note writing.
assets.subtitle_excerpt: time derivative of the state vector.
